column1: Timeline: Achaemenid Kings, Usurpers, Zoroastrianism, and Divine Appointment
Cyrus the Great: Recognized as divinely appointed by multiple traditions: Marduk (Babylonian), Yahweh (Hebrew Bible), and by Persian royal descent734.
Cambyses II, Bardiya, Darius I, Xerxes I: All claimed legitimacy through Achaemenid royal lineage and received divine sanction at investiture ceremonies, especially from Ahura Mazda35.
Darius I and Xerxes I: Explicitly stated in inscriptions that Ahura Mazda made them king235.
Gaumata: Not recognized as divinely appointed; considered an illegitimate usurper34.

The Achaemenid kings were not considered gods themselves (contrary to some Greek interpretations), but were viewed as divinely appointed, ruling by the favor of the supreme god (Ahura Mazda) and by royal blood3.
They were regarded as the gods’ representatives on earth, possessing farnah (divine glory/royal charisma)3.
Investiture ceremonies publicly affirmed the new king’s divine sanction and legitimacy, witnessed by nobility and representatives from across the empire5.
